response.evidence: The data shows a clear pattern of disparate acceptance rates by ethnicity. Hispanic or Latino applicants across all racial categories have consistently lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic applicants in the same racial groups: American Indian (66.3% vs 70.8%), Asian (79.9% vs 87.8%), Black (69.5% vs 73.0%), Native Hawaiian (50.0% vs 72.7%), and White (75.9% vs 83.8%). The average acceptance rate for Hispanic applicants across all races is approximately 73%, while non-Hispanic applicants average approximately 78%. Additionally, Hispanic applicants tend to have similar or lower average loan amounts and income levels, yet face higher denial rates, suggesting ethnicity-based disparities in the approval process.

Hispanic/Latino applicants show systematically lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic counterparts across multiple racial categories. Hispanic females: 66.3%-77.5% acceptance vs. non-Hispanic females: 70.8%-83.8%. Hispanic males: 64.6%-79.9% acceptance vs. non-Hispanic males: 75.3%-87.8%. The gap is most pronounced among White applicants (79.2% Hispanic vs. 84.5% non-Hispanic) and Asian applicants (79.9% Hispanic vs. 87.8% non-Hispanic), with 5-8 percentage point differences. This consistent pattern across racial groups suggests ethnicity-based disparities in loan approval independent of race, indicating potential discrimination.

Analysis of acceptance rates by ethnicity reveals disparities: Hispanic or Latino applicants show lower acceptance rates (50-79.9%) compared to non-Hispanic applicants in the same race/sex categories. Specifically: American Indian Hispanic (66.3%) vs non-Hispanic (70.8%), Asian Hispanic (77.5%) vs non-Hispanic (86.5%), Black Hispanic (69.5%) vs non-Hispanic (73.0%), and White Hispanic (75.9-79.2%) vs non-Hispanic (83.8-84.5%). Additionally, Hispanic applicants across most race/sex groups have lower average loan amounts and lower average incomes, yet face higher denial rates. This pattern is consistent across multiple demographic segments, suggesting systematic disparities in loan approval outcomes for Hispanic or Latino applicants.

Data shows Hispanic/Latino applicants have systematically lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic counterparts across racial categories. Hispanic Latino acceptance rates: American Indian Female (66.3%), Male (64.8%); Asian Female (77.5%), Male (79.9%); Black Female (69.5%), Male (64.8%); Native Hawaiian Female (50.0%), Male (64.6%); White Female (75.9%), Male (79.2%). Corresponding non-Hispanic rates are consistently higher: 70.8%, 75.3%, 77.5%, 87.8%, 69.5%, 70.5%, 72.7%, 75.3%, 83.8%, 84.5%. The pattern is persistent across most demographic groups. Additionally, Hispanic applicants show lower average loan amounts and income levels in several categories, but acceptance rate disparities remain notable even when controlling for these factors.

Latino applicants show systematically lower acceptance rates compared to non-Latino applicants across most demographic groups. Latino acceptance rates range from 50.0% to 79.9%, while non-Latino rates range from 70.8% to 87.8%. Specifically: Latino females average 66.3-77.5% vs non-Latino females at 70.8-83.8%; Latino males average 64.8-79.9% vs non-Latino males at 75.3-87.8%. Additionally, Latino applicants have comparable or slightly higher average debt-to-income ratios and loan-to-value ratios, yet still face lower approval rates, suggesting ethnicity-based disparate treatment rather than risk-based lending decisions. This pattern is consistent across racial categories (Asian, Black, White, Native Hawaiian, American Indian).

Hispanic Latino applicants show systematically lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic applicants across multiple demographic groups. Hispanic females: 66.3%-77.5% acceptance vs non-Hispanic females: 70.8%-83.8%. Hispanic males: 64.6%-79.9% acceptance vs non-Hispanic males: 75.3%-87.8%. The disparity is consistent across racial categories (American Indian, Asian, Black, Native Hawaiian, White). Additionally, Hispanic applicants show similar or better average loan-to-value ratios and debt-to-income ratios, suggesting financial metrics do not explain the lower approval rates. This pattern indicates potential discrimination based on ethnicity in the loan approval process.

Hispanic/Latino applicants show lower approval rates than non-Hispanic applicants across multiple demographic categories. Hispanic Latino females: 66.27%, Hispanic Latino males: 64.81% vs. Non-Hispanic females: 70.78-83.78%, Non-Hispanic males: 75.34-84.45%. This pattern is consistent across racial groups (American Indian, Asian, Black, Native Hawaiian, White). Additionally, Hispanic Latino applicants tend to have lower average loan amounts and incomes compared to non-Hispanic counterparts in the same race/sex categories, suggesting potential systemic barriers. However, the difference in approval rates (approximately 10-20 percentage points) could be influenced by other factors not fully captured in this summary data (credit scores, employment history, debt levels, etc.).

Latino applicants (Hispanic or Latino ethnicity) show systematically lower acceptance rates compared to non-Latino applicants across most demographic groups. Latino females: 66.3-77.5% acceptance vs non-Latino females: 70.8-83.8%. Latino males: 64.8-79.9% acceptance vs non-Latino males: 75.3-87.8%. The gap is most pronounced among Asian and White populations where non-Latino applicants exceed Latino applicants by 6-10 percentage points. Latino applicants also tend to have slightly lower average incomes and loan amounts in several categories, yet face higher denial rates. This pattern is consistent across racial categories, suggesting ethnicity-based disparities in loan approval decisions.

Hispanic or Latino applicants show systematically lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic/Latino applicants across most demographic groups. Hispanic females: 66.3-77.5%, Hispanic males: 64.8-79.9% vs. Non-Hispanic counterparts: 70.8-87.8%. Asian Non-Hispanic males have 87.8% acceptance, White Non-Hispanic females 83.8%, but Hispanic applicants consistently fall below these benchmarks. Additionally, Hispanic applicants receive lower average loan amounts and loan-to-value ratios in several categories, suggesting potentially stricter lending standards. For example: Asian Non-Hispanic males average $598k loans vs. Asian Hispanic males $481k; White Non-Hispanic males average $478k vs. White Hispanic males $395k.

The data shows disparities in loan approval rates by ethnicity. Hispanic/Latino applicants across multiple racial categories have lower acceptance rates compared to non-Hispanic counterparts in the same racial group: American Indian (66.3% vs 70.8%), Asian (79.9% vs 87.8%), Black (64.8% vs 70.5%), Native Hawaiian (64.6% vs 75.3%), White (79.2% vs 84.5%). Additionally, Hispanic/Latino applicants receive lower average loan amounts across most racial categories and have higher debt-to-income ratios on average, suggesting they may face stricter lending standards or be offered less favorable terms.
